ACS (Junior) P6 Science Preliminary Examination 2025

Booklet A — Multiple Choice (Q1–28, 2 marks each)

Question 1

2 marks

The following are characteristics of organism X.

  • A    It has wings.
  • B    It has three body parts.
  • C    It has three pairs of legs.
  • D    It reproduces by laying eggs.

Which characteristics can be used to conclude that organism X is an insect?

  1. A and B only
  2. A and D only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 3. Having three body parts (head, thorax, abdomen) and three pairs of legs are the defining characteristics of all insects. Wings are absent in some insects (e.g. ants), so A alone cannot confirm X is an insect. Egg-laying is shared by many non-insects, so D is not conclusive either.

Question 2

2 marks

Q, R, S and T represent different stages in the life cycle of a butterfly. The butterfly is able to reproduce at stage R.

At which stage, Q, R, S and T, is the butterfly considered a pest to farmers?

  1. Q
  2. R
  3. S
  4. T

Answer

Option 3 (S). Stage S is the caterpillar (larva). Caterpillars eat large amounts of plant leaves and are therefore pests to farmers. Q is the egg, R is the adult butterfly (feeds only on nectar), and T is the pupa, which does not feed.

Question 3

2 marks

Scientists found new plants on Island P near Island Y. The diagram shows the islands.

Which fruits A, B, C and/or D found on Island Y are most likely to be found on Island P?

  1. C only
  2. D only
  3. A, B and C only
  4. A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 4. All four fruits can be dispersed across the sea between the islands. Fruits dispersed by water float across; those with wings or parachutes travel by wind; fruits eaten by sea birds are carried across. All four seed-dispersal types work between nearby islands.

Question 4

2 marks

Process X takes place in the human female reproductive system.

In which part of the flower Z does a similar process occur?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 3 (C). Process X is fertilisation. In a flower, fertilisation takes place inside the ovary (part C), where the pollen tube delivers the male gamete to the ovule.

Question 5

2 marks

The table shows the main functions of organs W, X, Y and Z in the human digestive system.

FunctionOrgan(s)
Digestion of foodW, X, Z
Absorption of digested foodW
Absorption of waterY

What are organs W, X, Y and Z?

WXYZ
(1)StomachMouthLarge intestineSmall intestine
(2)StomachLarge intestineSmall intestineMouth
(3)Small intestineStomachLarge intestineMouth
(4)Small intestineMouthStomachLarge intestine

Answer

Option 3. W (small intestine) both digests food and absorbs digested nutrients. X (stomach) digests food with gastric juices. Y (large intestine) absorbs water from undigested food. Z (mouth) begins digestion by chewing and mixing food with saliva.

Question 6

2 marks

Three leaves from a plant were wrapped with different plastic bags of the same size and left in bright sunlight for some time.

LeafABC
Type of bagClear (sealed)Clear with holesBlack (sealed)

Which graph correctly represents the amount of food made by leaves A, B and C after five hours?

Answer

Option 3. Leaf B (holes) receives light and a fresh CO₂ supply — it photosynthesises most. Leaf A (sealed clear) receives light but CO₂ is depleted — less photosynthesis. Leaf C (black bag) receives no light — no photosynthesis. Correct ranking: B > A > C.

Question 7

2 marks

The diagram shows the different paths taken by food and water in a plant with parts A, B, C and D.

Which of the following represents A, B, C and D?

ABCD
(1)rootstemfruitfruit
(2)rootstemfruitleaf
(3)flowerleafrootstem
(4)stemrootleafflower

Answer

Option 1. Water is absorbed by the roots (A) and transported up through the stem (B) to all parts. Food (glucose) is made in the leaves and transported downward to the fruits (C and D — both fruits receive food via the food-path arrows).

Question 8

2 marks

Students investigated breathing rate by resting, then performing walk, jump and run exercises for 1 minute each and measuring breathing rate immediately after each. Which hypothesis is most suitable?

  1. The higher the exercise intensity, the higher the breathing rate.
  2. The lower the oxygen level in the air, the higher the breathing rate.
  3. The higher the surrounding air temperature, the higher the breathing rate.
  4. The longer the rest period between exercises, the lower the breathing rate.

Answer

Option 1. The experiment varies exercise intensity (walk < jump < run) and measures breathing rate — a perfect match for hypothesis 1. Options 2, 3 and 4 involve variables not tested in this experiment (oxygen level, temperature, rest duration).

Question 9

2 marks

Study the flowchart.

Where are cells P, Q and R taken from?

PQR
(1)Leaf of plantRoot of plantSkin of human
(2)Leaf of plantFlower of plantStem of plant
(3)Stem of plantSkin of humanRoot of plant
(4)Stem of plantRoot of plantRoot of plant

Answer

Option 1. The flowchart sorts cells by presence of a cell wall and chloroplasts. P (leaf) has both a cell wall and chloroplasts. Q (root) has a cell wall but no chloroplasts (roots do not photosynthesise). R (human skin) has neither — it is an animal cell.

Question 10

2 marks

Study the food web.

Which one of the following correctly shows the relationships among the organisms?

Prey onlyPredator onlyBoth prey and predator
(1)CEA, B, D and F
(2)CA and EB, D and F
(3)D and FA and EB and C
(4)D and FAB and C

Answer

Option 3. D and F are at the base of the food web — they are eaten but do not eat other organisms shown (prey only). A and E are top predators — they eat but are not eaten by others in the web (predators only). B and C occupy middle levels — they are both eaten and eat others (both prey and predator).

Question 11

2 marks

The graph shows the area covered by plant G in a garden. Animal X was introduced at week 4.

Which statements could explain the increase in plant G area after week 4?

  • A    Animal X helped plant G disperse its seeds.
  • B    Animal X preyed on animals that fed on plant G.
  • C    Animal X fed on plant G and plant G reproduced.
  • D    The waste from animal X helped plant G grow faster.
  1. A and D only
  2. B and C only
  3. A, B and D only
  4. B, C and D only

Answer

Option 3 (A, B and D only). A: Animal X dispersing seeds spreads plant G. B: Animal X eating the herbivores that previously ate plant G allows G to grow more. D: Waste from Animal X fertilises plant G. Statement C is self-contradictory — if X eats plant G, the area would decrease, not increase.

Question 12

2 marks

Three organisms R, S and T have characteristics that help them survive.

OrganismCharacteristic
RNeedle-shaped leaves
SThick, waxy cuticle
TAir spaces trapped in leaf stalk

Which correctly matches each characteristic to its function?

To breathe in waterTo reduce water lossTo float on water
(1)TSR
(2)TRS
(3)SRT
(4)STR

Answer

Option 2. T (air spaces in leaf stalk) provides a gas exchange channel that allows the plant to respire underwater. R (needle-shaped leaves) reduces surface area exposed to air, minimising water loss. S (thick waxy cuticle) repels water and helps the organism float on the water surface.

Question 13

2 marks

The PSI values of a town over five days: Day 1=46, Day 2=48, Day 3=123, Day 4=147, Day 5=144.

Which is most likely to have caused the sharp PSI increase from Day 2 to Day 4?

  1. A few trees were cut down.
  2. More forest fires happened nearby.
  3. More farmers started growing vegetables.
  4. More people switched from cars to electric cars.

Answer

Option 2. Forest fires produce large amounts of smoke, ash and fine particulates, causing a rapid spike in PSI from Good (48) to Unhealthy (123–147). Cutting a few trees, growing vegetables or switching to electric cars would not generate significant amounts of air pollutants.

Question 14

2 marks

Plants use water and a gas to make food through photosynthesis.

Which of the following best represents letters A, B, C, D and E during photosynthesis?

ABCDE
(1)HeatCarbon DioxideOxygenGlucoseChlorophyll
(2)SunlightOxygenCarbon DioxideGlucoseChlorophyll
(3)HeatOxygenOxygenWaterGlucose
(4)SunlightOxygenCarbon DioxideWaterGlucose

Answer

Option 4. Photosynthesis equation: CO₂ + H₂O + sunlight → Glucose + O₂. A = Sunlight (energy from above). B = Oxygen (released through leaves). C = Carbon dioxide (absorbed through leaves). D = Water (absorbed from soil via roots). E = Glucose (food produced and stored or used by the plant).

Question 15

2 marks

Water poured into set-up A only partially filled the flask; the same amount poured into set-up B filled it completely.

Which best explains this observation?

  1. Air in set-up B was compressed.
  2. Water in set-up B was compressed.
  3. The glass tube in set-up B allowed air to escape.
  4. Water in set-up A occupied the space in the flask so more water could not flow in.

Answer

Option 3. In set-up A, the air inside was trapped. As water entered, it compressed the air, which pushed back and blocked more water from flowing in. In set-up B, the glass tube provided an escape path — as water entered, air escaped through the tube, allowing all the water to fill the flask.

Question 16

2 marks

The diagram shows the water cycle.

Which correctly identifies X, Y and Z?

XYZ
(1)condensationevaporationsnow
(2)condensationcondensationcloud
(3)evaporationcondensationsnow
(4)evaporationcondensationcloud

Answer

Option 4. X = evaporation (liquid water from the ocean/lakes turns to water vapour and rises). Y = condensation (water vapour cools at higher altitude and condenses into tiny water droplets). Z = cloud (the visible mass of water droplets that forms through condensation).

Question 17

2 marks

Four identical containers P, Q, R and S were filled with the same amount of water and placed at different locations with varying conditions for one day.

Which graph shows the possible amount of water left in P, Q, R and S after one day?

Answer

Option 1. Evaporation is faster with more heat, more wind and a larger exposed surface area. The container with the hottest, windiest and most exposed conditions loses the most water. A covered container retains most of its water. Graph (1) correctly ranks the containers from most to least water remaining based on these conditions.

Question 18

2 marks

The diagram shows four circuits with identical batteries and bulbs.

Which bulb A, B, C or D will be the least bright?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 1. Bulb A is in a series circuit with the greatest number of bulbs, so the voltage is shared among more components and each bulb — including A — receives the least current. More bulbs in series means dimmer bulbs. Bulbs in parallel (or in shorter series) receive more current and shine brighter.

Question 19

2 marks

The diagram shows a toy with an electric circuit.

The table shows observations when the two switches are open/closed.

Switch 1Switch 2Observation
ClosedOpenLight only
OpenClosedMusic only
ClosedClosedMusic and light
OpenOpenNo music and light

Which circuit represents the toy?

Answer

Option 1. Because Switch 1 controls only the light and Switch 2 controls only the music — independently of each other — the light and speaker must be on separate parallel branches, each with its own switch. Circuit (1) shows this correct parallel arrangement.

Question 20

2 marks

The diagram shows three magnets with ends CD, EF and GH.

Which ends will repel?

  1. C and F
  2. C and H
  3. D and G
  4. D and H

Answer

Option 4. From the diagram, D and H are the same magnetic pole (both North or both South). Like poles repel each other. All other pairings listed are unlike poles that would attract.

Question 21

2 marks

Ali strokes a nail 50 times with a magnet as shown.

He then brought the nail near iron pins and it did not attract any. Which explains this observation?

  1. He stroked the nail in the wrong direction.
  2. The nail had become a temporary magnet.
  3. The nail is made of non-magnetic material.
  4. He used the North pole of the magnet to stroke the nail.

Answer

Option 3. If the nail does not attract iron pins even after 50 strokes, it cannot be magnetised — it is made of a non-magnetic material (e.g. copper or aluminium). Stroking direction and which pole is used only affect polarity. A temporary magnet (option 2) would still attract iron pins.

Question 22

2 marks

Marble R rolled from X. It was hit by marble S and eventually stopped at Y.

What can be concluded about the effects of forces?

  • A    A force can stop a moving object.
  • B    A force can change the size of an object.
  • C    A force can change the direction of a moving object.
  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 2 (A and C only). A: Friction eventually stopped marble R at Y. C: Marble S hitting R changed its direction. B is incorrect — forces change an object’s motion, not its size or shape.

Question 23

2 marks

Belle hung identical objects A (in air) and B (in sand) on identical springs, then lifted both upward by 5 cm.

Which statement is incorrect?

  1. More force is needed to lift object B.
  2. Gravitational force is acting on objects A and B.
  3. More gravitational force is acting on object A than on object B.
  4. There is more force acting downwards on object B due to the sand.

Answer

Option 3. Objects A and B are identical, so the gravitational force on each is the same. The extra force needed to lift B comes from the resistance of the sand, not from greater gravity. Options 1, 2 and 4 are all correct statements.

Question 24

2 marks

Sea turtle hatchlings are guided toward the sea by light reflected off the water. Nearby artificial lights can confuse them. Which streetlamp is least likely to confuse hatchlings?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 2 (B). Streetlamp B has a design (shielded/directed downward) that minimises light emitted toward the beach and ocean. Hatchlings are confused by light directed horizontally toward them. The other lamps emit light in directions that could mislead hatchlings into moving toward the lamp rather than the sea.

Question 25

2 marks

Dave attached wax pieces A, B and C to a metal frame with equal sides. When heated at one spot, wax melted in the order B, A, C.

At which spot W, X, Y or Z did Dave heat the frame?

  1. W
  2. X
  3. Y
  4. Z

Answer

Option 1 (W). Heat conducts outward along the metal frame from the heated spot. The wax closest (shortest conduction path) melts first. From spot W, the path to B is shortest, then to A, then to C, giving the observed melting order B → A → C.

Question 26

2 marks

Maverick placed four identical rods (all at 30°C) in crushed ice. After 5 minutes, their temperatures were: A=20°C, B=14°C, C=8°C, D=25°C.

What can Maverick conclude about heat conductivity?

  1. D is the best conductor of heat.
  2. C is the poorest conductor of heat.
  3. B is a better conductor of heat than C.
  4. C is a better conductor of heat than D.

Answer

Option 4. A better heat conductor transfers heat from the warm rod to the cold ice faster, so the rod cools more. Rod C dropped 22°C (best conductor). Rod D dropped only 5°C (poorest conductor). Therefore C conducts better than D. Options 1, 2 and 3 are all incorrect.

Question 27

2 marks

The diagram shows a metal spoon in a bowl of ice cream.

Which graph shows the changes in temperature of the metal spoon and the ice cream over time?

Answer

Option 2. The metal spoon starts at room temperature and cools rapidly as heat transfers to the cold ice cream. The ice cream starts near 0°C and changes very little (large thermal mass). Eventually both reach thermal equilibrium. Graph (2) shows the spoon temperature falling and converging with the roughly flat ice cream temperature line.

Question 28

2 marks

Jace lifted a pendulum bob to position A and let it swing to B, C and back to X.

Which graph shows how gravitational potential energy (GPE) changes as the bob swings from A to C to X?

Answer

Option 4. GPE is highest when the bob is at its highest points (A, C, X) and lowest at the bottom of the swing (B). As the bob swings A→B→C→B→X, GPE follows a wave: high→low→high→low→high. Graph (4) correctly shows this repeating pattern with peaks at A, C and X.

Booklet B — Open-Ended Questions (Q29–40)

Question 29

3 marks

Jinu measured heart rates of five children listening to slow-paced music, no music and fast-paced music.

ChildHeart Rate (per min)
SlowNoneFast
A7885110
B738398
C687488
D727990
E708092

(a) State the aim of Jinu’s experiment. [1]

(b) State the relationship between the pace of music and heart rate. [1]

(c) Suggest one improvement for more accurate results. [1]

Answer

(a) To find out how the pace of music affects the heart rate of children.
(b) As the pace of music increases, the heart rate of the children increases.
(c) Repeat the experiment at least three times and calculate the average heart rate to reduce the effect of random variation.

Question 30

3 marks

The diagram shows a pond community.

(a) How many populations are shown? [1]

(b) The graph shows oxygen in the pond after muddy water from a farm was washed in.

Explain why the oxygen level decreased over time. [1]

(c) Which organism is most likely to survive? Explain. [1]

Answer

(a) 4 populations.
(b) The mud blocked sunlight from reaching the aquatic plants. With less light, photosynthesis slowed and the plants produced less oxygen, reducing the dissolved oxygen level in the pond.
(c) The frog. Frogs are amphibians that can breathe through their lungs (or moist skin) on land when dissolved oxygen in the water becomes too low for survival.

Question 31

4 marks

Monkey A: short tail, light-brown thick fur. Monkey B: long tail, red thick fur.

(a) Which monkey survives better in a dry grassland (yellow grass, few trees) and in a rainforest (many tall trees)? Explain. [2]

(b) Both habitats cool by at least 5°C at night. Explain the advantage of thick fur for both monkeys. [1]

Answer

(a) Grassland: Monkey A. Its light-brown fur camouflages it among the yellow grass, reducing the chance of being spotted by predators.
Rainforest: Monkey B. Its long tail allows it to grip and swing between tall trees, helping it escape predators and navigate the canopy.
(b) Thick fur traps a layer of air close to the skin. Air is a poor conductor of heat, so less body heat is lost to the cooler surroundings, keeping both monkeys warm at night.

Question 32

5 marks

James observed flower C: no scent, small dull petals, anthers and feathery stigma hang outside petals.

(a) State how flower C is likely pollinated. [1]

(b) James also observed flowers D and E.

(i) Which flower, D or E, can develop into a fruit? Explain. [1]

(ii) Bees visit both flowers. State how bees and flowers benefit from each other. [2]

Answer

(a) Wind pollination.
(b)(i) Flower D. Only flower D has a stigma and an ovary containing ovules. After pollination and fertilisation, the ovary develops into a fruit. Flower E lacks a stigma and ovary and cannot be fertilised.
(b)(ii) Bees transfer pollen from the anthers of flower E to the stigma of flower D, enabling pollination (flowers benefit from reproduction). The bees collect nectar from both flowers as food (bees benefit).

Question 33

3 marks

Minghe bought hot and iced coffee in paper cups with clear plastic lids and a plastic coating inside.

(a) Left hand (hot coffee) felt warm; right hand (iced coffee) felt cold. Explain. [1]

(b) He could see through the clear lid but not through the paper cup. Explain. [1]

(c) He thinks the plastic coating prevents leakage. Suggest a test for his idea. [1]

Answer

(a) His left hand gained heat from the hotter coffee, making it feel warm. His right hand lost heat to the colder drink, making it feel cold. Heat always flows from the warmer object to the cooler object.
(b) The clear plastic lid is transparent — it allows light to pass through so he can see the coffee inside. The paper cup is opaque — it does not allow light to pass through, so he cannot see through it.
(c) Pour equal amounts of water at the same temperature into two cups: one with the plastic coating inside and one without. Observe whether the uncoated cup leaks. If it leaks but the coated cup does not, the coating prevents leakage.

Question 34

4 marks

A security guard needs to control two ‘slow down’ sign bulbs at different gates independently — one switch per bulb.

(a) Complete the circuit diagram so it works as described. [3]

(b) State one advantage of connecting the bulbs this way. [1]

Answer

(a) The two bulbs must be connected in parallel, each with its own switch in series on its branch, all sharing one battery. This lets each switch control its bulb independently.
(b) When one bulb blows or is switched off, the other bulb continues to work normally (it is on a separate branch and is unaffected).

Question 35

6 marks

Jarrell investigated which glass A or B keeps water cold longer. Both are the same material.

He measured temperature every 10 minutes for 50 minutes.

(a) Match lines X and Y to glasses A and B. [1]

LineGlass
X
Y

(b) State two variables Jarrell must keep constant for a fair test. [1]

(c) State the temperature increase for water in glass A when it reached room temperature. [1]

(d) Are double-layered or single-layered glass windows more suitable for Singapore houses (28°C inside, 32°C outside)? Explain. [2]

Answer

(a) X → Glass B (slower temperature rise, double-layered). Y → Glass A (faster temperature rise, single-layered).
(b) Any two: starting temperature of the water; volume of water in each glass; surrounding room temperature.
(c) 20°C (from ~5°C starting temperature to ~25°C room temperature).
(d) Double-layered. The trapped air between the two glass panes is a poor conductor of heat, slowing the transfer of heat from the hotter outside (32°C) to the cooler inside (28°C), keeping the house cooler.

Question 36

3 marks

An eco-friendly playground uses children’s movements and solar panels to generate electricity via a wheel connected to a generator.

(a) Where does Elliott’s energy come from? [1]

(b) Fill in the energy conversions when a child runs on the wheel. [1]
_____ energy → _____ energy → _____ energy

(c) State one advantage of the solar panels. [1]

Answer

(a) The food he ate (chemical potential energy stored in food).
(b) Kinetic energy → Electrical energy → Light energy.
(c) Solar energy is a renewable source / does not produce harmful gases or pollution / reduces dependence on fossil fuels. (Accept any one.)

Question 37

5 marks

John built a seesaw with ducks carrying iron rings of different masses, connected to a circuit with an iron bar that becomes an electromagnet when the switch is closed.

(a) When John closed the switch, both ducks moved up and down continuously. Explain. [2]

(b) What could John do to make the ducks move faster? [1]

(c) John replaced the 5 g iron ring with a 5 g permanent magnet ring. Duck B moved up but did not return. Explain. [2]

Answer

(a) When the switch is closed, current flows and the iron bar becomes an electromagnet, attracting the iron ring under Duck A and pulling Duck A down. This separates the metal contacts, breaking the circuit. The electromagnet loses its magnetism. Duck B (heavier ring) then falls due to greater gravitational force, reconnecting the contacts. This cycle repeats continuously.
(b) Increase the number of batteries (higher voltage) or increase the number of coils around the iron bar (stronger electromagnet). Accept either one.
(c) When Duck B was pulled up and the circuit broke, the electromagnet lost its magnetism. However, the permanent magnet ring under Duck A continued to attract the iron ring of Duck B even without current. This permanent attraction kept Duck B raised and prevented it from returning to its original position.

Question 38

4 marks

Meiling measured the force to pull blocks of different masses across a flat surface using a spring balance.

(a) State what Meiling could conclude from her experiment. [1]

(b) MRT train wheels show greater wear when more passengers travel. Explain how increasing passenger numbers causes more frequent breakdowns.

[2]

Answer

(a) The greater the mass of the block, the greater the force (friction) needed to pull it across the surface.
(b) More passengers increase the total mass of the train. A greater mass results in a greater frictional force between the metal wheels and tracks. This greater friction causes faster wear and tear on the wheels, leading to more frequent breakdowns.

Question 39

4 marks

Siti packed clothes into a bag and sealed it.

(a) Identify the states of matter in the sealed bag. [1]

(b) Siti wants to find the mass of air that can be removed by pressing the bag.

(i) Describe the steps using an electronic balance. [2]

(ii) Will the volume of the bag decrease, increase or stay the same after air is removed? Explain using the physical property of air. [1]

Answer

(a) Solid (clothes) and gas (air inside the bag).
(b)(i) Step 1: Measure and record the mass of the sealed bag with clothes (M₁) using an electronic balance. Step 2: Press the bag firmly to expel as much air as possible, then reseal it. Step 3: Measure and record the new mass (M₂). Step 4: Mass of air removed = M₁ − M₂.
(b)(ii) Decrease. Air is matter and occupies space. When air is removed from the bag, there is less matter inside to occupy space, so the bag shrinks and its total volume decreases.

Question 40

4 marks

(a) State what evaporation is. [1]

Mr Tan placed his wet clasped hands under a wall-mounted hand dryer that produces hot air at high speed.

Mr Tan noticed his hands dried faster with the hand dryer.

(b)(i) State two reasons the hand dryer dries hands faster. [1]

(b)(ii) Explain your answer in (b)(i). [1]

(b)(iii) Mr Tan spread out his hands instead of clasping them. Will his hands dry faster or slower? Explain. [1]

Answer

(a) Evaporation is the process where liquid water on the surface changes into water vapour (gas) at any temperature, as surface particles gain enough energy to escape.
(b)(i) The hot air supplies heat; the high-speed airflow provides wind.
(b)(ii) Heat gives water molecules on the hands more energy to escape as vapour, increasing the rate of evaporation. The fast-moving air (wind) carries water vapour away from the hands, preventing the air near the skin from becoming saturated and allowing evaporation to continue at a high rate.
(b)(iii) Faster. Spreading out the hands increases the exposed surface area. A larger surface area allows more water molecules to evaporate at the same time, increasing the rate of evaporation and drying the hands more quickly.
